explicit_text: I ran away from my home in South Chicago when I was thirteen to become a freshwater sailor. Later, during the winter of 1911, I worked at the Illinois Steel Company in South Chicago before returning to the lakes. South Chicago has always been a significant place in my life, both as home and as a maritime hub.

explicit_text: I have vivid memories of being in Duluth around Thanksgiving when Jack Mc Nellie and I shipped on the yacht Salt Lake City to Brooklyn. explicit_text: The Salt Lake City was a yacht we took through the Great Lakes to Brooklyn, New York, around Thanksgiving time. explicit_text: When I was thirteen, I stowed away on the Wings of St. Joe, a small sailing vessel running between Benton Harbor, Michigan and South Chicago. It carried fruit and vegetables from Michigan and poultry back to Chicago. On one trip, we were wrecked on a sandbar during a savage gale, but the skipper saved me and the U.S. Coast Guard helped refloat the vessel after the storm.

explicit_text: Off Whitefish Point in Lake Superior, I had a dangerous experience aboard the Vulcan during a heavy snowstorm. Our skipper tried to navigate between a small rocky island and the mainland, but a crosswind pushed us far off course. We ended up near the Canadian shore instead of the island, narrowly avoiding disaster in that treacherous area.

explicit_text: At the start of World War I, I was a wheelman on a lake boat but decided to quit in Duluth and enlist with the Canadians. We left Halifax in a transport bound for England with about 5,000 men. During the voyage, our ship was torpedoed by a German submarine, but we managed to beach the vessel and were rescued without loss of life.
